title_en: Ahmad az-Zarqa and the Modern Study of Legal Maxims
title_id: Ahmad al-Zarqa dan Kajian Kaidah-Kaidah Hukum di Era Modern
content_id:
Syekh Ahmad ibn Muhammad al-Zarqa al-Halabi (1869–1938 M) adalah seorang faqih Hanafi dan ulama terkemuka dari Damaskus pada akhir era Ottoman dan awal era mandat. Lahir dalam keluarga ulama Islam yang terkemuka — ayahnya adalah faqih terkenal Muhammad al-Zarqa al-Halabi — Ahmad al-Zarqa menerima pendidikan klasik yang menyeluruh sebelum menjadi salah satu otoritas keagamaan paling dihormati dalam masyarakat Suriah selama periode transformasi politik dan intelektual yang mendalam.

Syarh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah adalah komentar al-Zarqa atas sembilan puluh sembilan kaidah hukum (qawa'id) yang terdapat dalam bagian pertama Majalla Ottoman — kode sipil Kekaisaran Ottoman yang tetap berlaku di beberapa negara Arab hingga jauh ke dalam abad kedua puluh. Dengan menulis komentar terperinci atas kaidah-kaidah ini, al-Zarqa mencapai dua hal sekaligus: ia menjelaskan prinsip-prinsip dasar Majalla dengan cara yang membuatnya mudah dipahami oleh pengacara dan hakim terlatih yang bekerja di bawah tatanan hukum baru, dan ia menghubungkan prinsip-prinsip ini kembali ke akar-akarnya dalam fikih Islam klasik, khususnya tradisi Hanafi dari mana prinsip-prinsip tersebut diambil.

Metodologi Syarh al-Qawa'id adalah mengambil setiap kaidah dari sembilan puluh sembilan secara berurutan, menjelaskan maknanya dalam bahasa Arab yang jelas, menelusuri dasar buktinya dalam Al-Quran, Sunnah, dan penalaran yuridis klasik, serta mengilustrasikan penerapannya dengan contoh-contoh hukum konkret yang diambil dari seluruh cakupan hukum Islam. Al-Zarqa menulis untuk pembaca yang mencakup bukan hanya ulama tradisional tetapi juga pengacara yang terlatih secara profesional yang beroperasi di pengadilan yang memadukan unsur-unsur hukum Islam dan hukum sipil, dan uraiannya mencerminkan khalayak ganda ini.

Karya ini menjadi rujukan standar di sekolah-sekolah hukum Arab pada abad kedua puluh, menjembatani kesenjangan antara pendidikan hukum Islam klasik dan sistem-sistem hukum yang memodernisasi negara-negara pasca-Ottoman. Putra al-Zarqa, Mustafa Ahmad al-Zarqa, sendiri merupakan ulama hukum Islam besar yang berkontribusi secara ekstensif pada kodifikasi hukum Islam dan pengembangan keuangan Islam — pekerjaan yang dibangun langsung di atas fondasi yang telah diletakkan ayahnya dalam Syarh al-Qawa'id.

Di luar konten hukum teknisnya, Syarh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah mewakili momen penting dalam pertemuan antara fikih Islam klasik dan dorongan-dorongan modernisasi abad kesembilan belas dan kedua puluh. Pendekatan al-Zarqa — terlibat serius dengan tradisi yang diwarisi sambil membuatnya mudah dipahami dan dapat diterapkan secara praktis — menjadi model bagi generasi-generasi selanjutnya dari pembaru hukum Islam.
content_en:
Shaykh Ahmad ibn Muhammad az-Zarqa al-Halabi (1869–1938 CE) was a leading Hanafi jurist and scholar of Damascus in the late Ottoman and early mandate periods. Born into a distinguished family of Islamic scholarship — his father was the noted jurist Muhammad az-Zarqa al-Halabi — Ahmad az-Zarqa received a thorough classical education before becoming one of the most respected religious authorities in Syrian society during a period of profound political and intellectual transformation.

Sharh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah is az-Zarqa's commentary on the ninety-nine legal maxims (qawa'id) contained in the first section of the Ottoman Majalla — the civil code of the Ottoman Empire that remained in force in several Arab countries well into the twentieth century. By writing a detailed commentary on these maxims, az-Zarqa accomplished two things simultaneously: he explained the Majalla's foundational principles in a way that made them accessible to trained lawyers and judges working under the new legal order, and he connected these principles back to their roots in classical Islamic jurisprudence, particularly the Hanafi tradition from which they were drawn.

The methodology of Sharh al-Qawa'id is to take each of the ninety-nine maxims in sequence, explain its meaning in clear Arabic, trace its evidential basis in the Quran, Sunnah, and classical juristic reasoning, and illustrate its application with concrete legal examples drawn from across the breadth of Islamic law. Az-Zarqa was writing for an audience that included not only traditional scholars but also professionally trained lawyers operating in courts that mixed Islamic and civil law elements, and his exposition reflects this dual audience.

The work became a standard reference in Arab law schools in the twentieth century, bridging the gap between classical Islamic legal education and the modernizing legal systems of the post-Ottoman states. Az-Zarqa's son, Mustafa Ahmad az-Zarqa, was himself a major Islamic legal scholar who contributed extensively to the codification of Islamic law and to the development of Islamic finance — work that built directly on the foundation his father had laid in Sharh al-Qawa'id.

Beyond its technical legal content, Sharh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah represents an important moment in the encounter between classical Islamic jurisprudence and the modernizing impulses of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Az-Zarqa's approach — engaging seriously with the inherited tradition while making it accessible and practically applicable — became a model for subsequent generations of Islamic legal reformers.
content_ar: Shaykh أحمد ibn محمد ﷺ az-Zarqa al-Halabi (1869–1938 CE) was a leading الحنفي jurist and scholar of Damascus in the late Ottoman and early mandate periods. Born into a distinguished الأسرة of Islamic scholarship — his الأب was the noted jurist محمد ﷺ az-Zarqa al-Halabi — أحمد az-Zarqa received a thorough الكلاسيكي education قبل becoming one of the most respected religious authorities in Syrian المجتمع خلال a period of profound political and intellectual transformation. Sharh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah is az-Zarqa's commentary on the ninety-nine legal maxims (qawa'id) contained in the أولاً الفصل of the Ottoman Majalla — the civil code of the Ottoman Empire that remained in force in several Arab countries well into the twentieth century. By writing a detailed commentary on these maxims, az-Zarqa accomplished two things simultaneously: أوضح the Majalla's foundational principles in a الطريق that made them accessible to trained lawyers and judges working under the new legal order, and he connected these principles back to their roots in الكلاسيكي Islamic jurisprudence, particularly the الحنفي التراث from which they were drawn. The methodology of Sharh al-Qawa'id is to take each of the ninety-nine maxims in sequence, explain its meaning in clear Arabic, trace its evidential basis in the القرآن الكريم, السنة, and الكلاسيكي juristic reasoning, and illustrate its application with concrete legal examples drawn from across the breadth of Islamic law. Az-Zarqa was writing for an audience that included not only التقليدي العلماء but أيضاً professionally trained lawyers operating in courts that mixed Islamic and civil law elements, and his exposition reflects this dual audience. The work became a standard reference in Arab law schools in the twentieth century, bridging the gap between الكلاسيكي Islamic legal education and the modernizing legal systems of the post-Ottoman states. Az-Zarqa's son, Mustafa أحمد az-Zarqa, was himself a major Islamic legal scholar who contributed extensively to the codification of Islamic law and to the development of Islamic finance — work that built directly on the foundation his الأب had laid in Sharh al-Qawa'id. Beyond its technical legal content, Sharh al-Qawa'id al-Fiqhiyyah represents an المهم moment in the encounter between الكلاسيكي Islamic jurisprudence and the modernizing impulses of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Az-Zarqa's approach — engaging seriously with the inherited التراث بينما making it accessible and practically applicable — became a model for subsequent generations of Islamic legal reformers.
